lines: Sir, I love you more than word can wield the
matter,
Dearer than eyesight, space, and liberty,
Beyond what can be valued, rich or rare,
No less than life, with grace, health, beauty, honor;
As much as child e'er loved, or father found;
A love that makes breath poor, and speech unable.
Beyond all manner of so much I love you.
fullText: Sir, I love you more than word can wield the matter, Dearer than eyesight, space, and liberty, Beyond what can be valued, rich or rare, No less than life, with grace, health, beauty, honor; As much as child e'er loved, or father found; A love that makes breath poor, and speech unable. Beyond all manner of so much I love you.

lines: Let your study
Be to content your lord, who hath received you
At Fortune's alms. You have obedience scanted
And well are worth the want that you have wanted.
fullText: Let your study Be to content your lord, who hath received you At Fortune's alms. You have obedience scanted And well are worth the want that you have wanted.

lines: Sister, it is not little I have to say of what
most nearly appertains to us both. I think our
father will hence tonight.
fullText: Sister, it is not little I have to say of what most nearly appertains to us both. I think our father will hence tonight.

lines: You see how full of changes his age is; the
observation we have made of it hath not been
little. He always loved our sister most, and with
what poor judgment he hath now cast her off
appears too grossly.
fullText: You see how full of changes his age is; the observation we have made of it hath not been little. He always loved our sister most, and with what poor judgment he hath now cast her off appears too grossly.

lines: The best and soundest of his time hath been
but rash. Then must we look from his age to
receive not alone the imperfections of long-engraffed
condition, but therewithal the unruly waywardness
that infirm and choleric years bring with
them.
fullText: The best and soundest of his time hath been but rash. Then must we look from his age to receive not alone the imperfections of long-engraffed condition, but therewithal the unruly waywardness that infirm and choleric years bring with them.

lines: There is further compliment of leave-taking
between France and him. Pray you, let us sit
together. If our father carry authority with such
disposition as he bears, this last surrender of his will
but offend us.
fullText: There is further compliment of leave-taking between France and him. Pray you, let us sit together. If our father carry authority with such disposition as he bears, this last surrender of his will but offend us.

lines: We must do something, and i' th' heat.
fullText: We must do something, and i' th' heat.

lines: Did my father strike my gentleman for chiding
of his Fool?
fullText: Did my father strike my gentleman for chiding of his Fool?

lines: By day and night he wrongs me. Every hour
He flashes into one gross crime or other
That sets us all at odds. I'll not endure it.
His knights grow riotous, and himself upbraids us
On every trifle. When he returns from hunting,
I will not speak with him. Say I am sick.
If you come slack of former services,
You shall do well. The fault of it I'll answer.
fullText: By day and night he wrongs me. Every hour He flashes into one gross crime or other That sets us all at odds. I'll not endure it. His knights grow riotous, and himself upbraids us On every trifle. When he returns from hunting, I will not speak with him. Say I am sick. If you come slack of former services, You shall do well. The fault of it I'll answer.

lines: Put on what weary negligence you please,
You and your fellows. I'd have it come to question.
If he distaste it, let him to my sister,
Whose mind and mine I know in that are one,
Not to be overruled. Idle old man
That still would manage those authorities
That he hath given away. Now, by my life,
Old fools are babes again and must be used
With checks as flatteries, when they are seen
abused.
Remember what I have said.
fullText: Put on what weary negligence you please, You and your fellows. I'd have it come to question. If he distaste it, let him to my sister, Whose mind and mine I know in that are one, Not to be overruled. Idle old man That still would manage those authorities That he hath given away. Now, by my life, Old fools are babes again and must be used With checks as flatteries, when they are seen abused. Remember what I have said.

lines: And let his knights have colder looks among you.
What grows of it, no matter. Advise your fellows so.
I would breed from hence occasions, and I shall,
That I may speak. I'll write straight to my sister
To hold my very course. Prepare for dinner.
fullText: And let his knights have colder looks among you. What grows of it, no matter. Advise your fellows so. I would breed from hence occasions, and I shall, That I may speak. I'll write straight to my sister To hold my very course. Prepare for dinner.

lines: Not only, sir, this your all-licensed Fool,
But other of your insolent retinue
Do hourly carp and quarrel, breaking forth
In rank and not-to-be-endured riots. Sir,
I had thought by making this well known unto you
To have found a safe redress, but now grow fearful,
By what yourself too late have spoke and done,
That you protect this course and put it on
By your allowance; which if you should, the fault
Would not 'scape censure, nor the redresses sleep
Which in the tender of a wholesome weal
Might in their working do you that offense,
Which else were shame, that then necessity
Will call discreet proceeding.
fullText: Not only, sir, this your all-licensed Fool, But other of your insolent retinue Do hourly carp and quarrel, breaking forth In rank and not-to-be-endured riots. Sir, I had thought by making this well known unto you To have found a safe redress, but now grow fearful, By what yourself too late have spoke and done, That you protect this course and put it on By your allowance; which if you should, the fault Would not 'scape censure, nor the redresses sleep Which in the tender of a wholesome weal Might in their working do you that offense, Which else were shame, that then necessity Will call discreet proceeding.

lines: I would you would make use of your good wisdom,
Whereof I know you are fraught, and put away
These dispositions which of late transport you
From what you rightly are.
fullText: I would you would make use of your good wisdom, Whereof I know you are fraught, and put away These dispositions which of late transport you From what you rightly are.

lines: This admiration, sir, is much o' th' savor
Of other your new pranks. I do beseech you
To understand my purposes aright.
As you are old and reverend, should be wise.
Here do you keep a hundred knights and squires,
Men so disordered, so debauched and bold,
That this our court, infected with their manners,
Shows like a riotous inn. Epicurism and lust
Makes it more like a tavern or a brothel
Than a graced palace. The shame itself doth speak
For instant remedy. Be then desired,
By her that else will take the thing she begs,
A little to disquantity your train,
And the remainders that shall still depend
To be such men as may besort your age,
Which know themselves and you.
fullText: This admiration, sir, is much o' th' savor Of other your new pranks. I do beseech you To understand my purposes aright. As you are old and reverend, should be wise. Here do you keep a hundred knights and squires, Men so disordered, so debauched and bold, That this our court, infected with their manners, Shows like a riotous inn. Epicurism and lust Makes it more like a tavern or a brothel Than a graced palace. The shame itself doth speak For instant remedy. Be then desired, By her that else will take the thing she begs, A little to disquantity your train, And the remainders that shall still depend To be such men as may besort your age, Which know themselves and you.

lines: You strike my people, and your disordered rabble
Make servants of their betters.
fullText: You strike my people, and your disordered rabble Make servants of their betters.

lines: Never afflict yourself to know more of it,
But let his disposition have that scope
As dotage gives it.
fullText: Never afflict yourself to know more of it, But let his disposition have that scope As dotage gives it.

lines: Do you mark that?
fullText: Do you mark that?

lines: Pray you, content.--What, Oswald, ho!--
You, sir, more knave than Fool, after your master.
fullText: Pray you, content.--What, Oswald, ho!-- You, sir, more knave than Fool, after your master.

lines: This man hath had good counsel. A hundred
knights!
'Tis politic and safe to let him keep
At point a hundred knights! Yes, that on every
dream,
Each buzz, each fancy, each complaint, dislike,
He may enguard his dotage with their powers
And hold our lives in mercy.--Oswald, I say!
fullText: This man hath had good counsel. A hundred knights! 'Tis politic and safe to let him keep At point a hundred knights! Yes, that on every dream, Each buzz, each fancy, each complaint, dislike, He may enguard his dotage with their powers And hold our lives in mercy.--Oswald, I say!

lines: Safer than trust too far.
Let me still take away the harms I fear,
Not fear still to be taken. I know his heart.
What he hath uttered I have writ my sister.
If she sustain him and his hundred knights
When I have showed th' unfitness--
fullText: Safer than trust too far. Let me still take away the harms I fear, Not fear still to be taken. I know his heart. What he hath uttered I have writ my sister. If she sustain him and his hundred knights When I have showed th' unfitness--

lines: Take you some company and away to horse.
Inform her full of my particular fear,
And thereto add such reasons of your own
As may compact it more. Get you gone,
And hasten your return. No, no, my
lord,
This milky gentleness and course of yours,
Though I condemn not, yet, under pardon,
You are much more at task for want of wisdom
Than praised for harmful mildness.
fullText: Take you some company and away to horse. Inform her full of my particular fear, And thereto add such reasons of your own As may compact it more. Get you gone, And hasten your return. No, no, my lord, This milky gentleness and course of yours, Though I condemn not, yet, under pardon, You are much more at task for want of wisdom Than praised for harmful mildness.

lines: Nay, then--
fullText: Nay, then--

lines: Why not by th' hand, sir? How have I offended?
All's not offense that indiscretion finds
And dotage terms so.
fullText: Why not by th' hand, sir? How have I offended? All's not offense that indiscretion finds And dotage terms so.

lines: At your choice, sir.
fullText: At your choice, sir.

lines: Why might not you, my lord, receive attendance
From those that she calls servants, or from mine?
fullText: Why might not you, my lord, receive attendance From those that she calls servants, or from mine?

lines: Hear me, my lord.
What need you five-and-twenty, ten, or five,
To follow in a house where twice so many
Have a command to tend you?
fullText: Hear me, my lord. What need you five-and-twenty, ten, or five, To follow in a house where twice so many Have a command to tend you?

lines: 'Tis his own blame hath put himself from rest,
And must needs taste his folly.
fullText: 'Tis his own blame hath put himself from rest, And must needs taste his folly.

lines: So am I purposed. Where is my lord of Gloucester?
fullText: So am I purposed. Where is my lord of Gloucester?

lines: My lord, entreat him by no means to stay.
fullText: My lord, entreat him by no means to stay.

lines: Pluck out his eyes.
fullText: Pluck out his eyes.

lines: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.
fullText: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.

lines: Welcome, my lord. I marvel our mild husband
Not met us on the way.
fullText: Welcome, my lord. I marvel our mild husband Not met us on the way.

lines: Then shall you go no further.
It is the cowish terror of his spirit,
That dares not undertake. He'll not feel wrongs
Which tie him to an answer. Our wishes on the way
May prove effects. Back, Edmund, to my brother.
Hasten his musters and conduct his powers.
I must change names at home and give the distaff
Into my husband's hands. This trusty servant
Shall pass between us. Ere long you are like to
hear--
If you dare venture in your own behalf--
A mistress's command. Wear this; spare speech.
Decline your head. This kiss, if it
durst speak,
Would stretch thy spirits up into the air.
Conceive, and fare thee well.
fullText: Then shall you go no further. It is the cowish terror of his spirit, That dares not undertake. He'll not feel wrongs Which tie him to an answer. Our wishes on the way May prove effects. Back, Edmund, to my brother. Hasten his musters and conduct his powers. I must change names at home and give the distaff Into my husband's hands. This trusty servant Shall pass between us. Ere long you are like to hear-- If you dare venture in your own behalf-- A mistress's command. Wear this; spare speech. Decline your head. This kiss, if it durst speak, Would stretch thy spirits up into the air. Conceive, and fare thee well.

lines: My most dear
Gloucester!
O, the difference of man and man!
To thee a woman's services are due;
My fool usurps my body.
fullText: My most dear Gloucester! O, the difference of man and man! To thee a woman's services are due; My fool usurps my body.

lines: I have been worth the whistle.
fullText: I have been worth the whistle.

lines: No more. The text is foolish.
fullText: No more. The text is foolish.

lines: Milk-livered man,
That bear'st a cheek for blows, a head for wrongs;
Who hast not in thy brows an eye discerning
Thine honor from thy suffering; that not know'st
Fools do those villains pity who are punished
Ere they have done their mischief. Where's thy
drum?
France spreads his banners in our noiseless land,
With plumed helm thy state begins to threat,
Whilst thou, a moral fool, sits still and cries
"Alack, why does he so?"
fullText: Milk-livered man, That bear'st a cheek for blows, a head for wrongs; Who hast not in thy brows an eye discerning Thine honor from thy suffering; that not know'st Fools do those villains pity who are punished Ere they have done their mischief. Where's thy drum? France spreads his banners in our noiseless land, With plumed helm thy state begins to threat, Whilst thou, a moral fool, sits still and cries "Alack, why does he so?"

lines: O vain fool!
fullText: O vain fool!

lines: Marry, your manhood, mew--
fullText: Marry, your manhood, mew--

lines: One way I like this well.
But being widow and my Gloucester with her
May all the building in my fancy pluck
Upon my hateful life. Another way
The news is not so tart.--I'll read, and answer.
fullText: One way I like this well. But being widow and my Gloucester with her May all the building in my fancy pluck Upon my hateful life. Another way The news is not so tart.--I'll read, and answer.

lines: I had rather lose the battle than that sister
Should loosen him and me.
fullText: I had rather lose the battle than that sister Should loosen him and me.

lines: Combine together 'gainst the enemy,
For these domestic and particular broils
Are not the question here.
fullText: Combine together 'gainst the enemy, For these domestic and particular broils Are not the question here.

lines: No.
fullText: No.

lines: Oho, I know the riddle.--I will go.
fullText: Oho, I know the riddle.--I will go.

lines: Not so hot.
In his own grace he doth exalt himself
More than in your addition.
fullText: Not so hot. In his own grace he doth exalt himself More than in your addition.

lines: That were the most if he should husband you.
fullText: That were the most if he should husband you.

lines: Holla, holla!
That eye that told you so looked but asquint.
fullText: Holla, holla! That eye that told you so looked but asquint.

lines: Mean you to enjoy him?
fullText: Mean you to enjoy him?

lines: An interlude!
fullText: An interlude!

lines: If not, I'll ne'er trust medicine.
fullText: If not, I'll ne'er trust medicine.

lines: This is practice, Gloucester.
By th' law of war, thou wast not bound to answer
An unknown opposite. Thou art not vanquished,
But cozened and beguiled.
fullText: This is practice, Gloucester. By th' law of war, thou wast not bound to answer An unknown opposite. Thou art not vanquished, But cozened and beguiled.

lines: Say if I do; the laws are mine, not thine.
Who can arraign me for 't?
fullText: Say if I do; the laws are mine, not thine. Who can arraign me for 't?

lines: Ask me not what I know.
fullText: Ask me not what I know.
